footer.description: Security, control, and compliance for your network infrastructure.

hero.subtitle: Simplify the management of court-ordered blocklists and protect your infrastructure with preventative blocking, legal compliance, and network security.

features.title: Key Features
features.subtitle: A complete platform of tools to ensure the security and compliance of your operation.

dnsBlocking.title: Global DNS Blocking
dnsBlocking.desc: Real-time interception and redirection of DNS requests, ensuring immediate compliance with court orders.

ipFiltering.title: IP Filtering
ipFiltering.desc: Efficient traffic blocking for malicious or unauthorized IPs directly at your network edge.

compliance.title: Compliance Automation
compliance.desc: Automate the blocking process required by judicial bodies, avoiding fines and sanctions.

audit.title: Dynamic Integration
audit.desc: Integrate your DNS servers and BGP edge routers with our platform to keep your blocklists dynamically updated.

cta.title: Ready to take control?
cta.subtitle: Ensure security and compliance automatically for your infrastructure.

whyImportant.title: Why is Preventive Blocking Crucial?
whyImportant.subtitle: In the current cybersecurity and regulation landscape, internet providers and companies face growing challenges. Kratox simplifies this complexity.

legal.title: Legal Compliance
legal.desc: Quickly meet court orders to block illegal sites and services (piracy, unregulated gambling, etc.) without impacting your operation.

security.title: Network Security
security.desc: Prevent infected devices on your network from communicating with command and control (C2) servers through effective DNS blocking.

integrity.title: Infrastructure Integrity
integrity.desc: Protect your network by blocking prohibited or unwanted IP addresses and domains.

docs.title: Documentation and Resources
docs.subtitle: Access our complete guides to integrate Kratox into your existing infrastructure, whether MikroTik, Cisco, Juniper, or Linux.

items.question: How can I block addresses?
items.answer: You can block network addresses manually in different ways: by typing the IP or DNS address directly, by pasting excerpts from legal documents requesting the block, or by uploading documents containing blocking orders.

items.question: What happens if the PDF contains legitimate addresses cited as examples? Will the AI suggest blocking them?
items.answer: Our AI analyzes the context of each sentence. If a domain is cited as a "victim" or as an example of a legitimate site, it is ignored. In any case, the review step ensures you have the final say before any action is taken.

items.question: Is there a review step before blocking?
items.answer: Yes. After analyzing the text or document, the Artificial Intelligence extracts the identified addresses and presents a list to the user. The user then reviews and selects which addresses to block before the rule is applied.

items.question: Once I import a court order, is the blocklist on my routers updated automatically?
items.answer: Kratox.io generates a dynamic feed in compatible formats (such as MikroTik Address Lists or BIND zone files). You configure your router to query that link periodically, and as soon as you approve a new domain on the platform, all your devices update automatically.

items.question: How can I connect the blocklist to my devices?
items.answer: You can generate a dynamic blocklist link directly on the platform. By connecting that link to your device (such as a router, DNS server, or firewall), the list will be updated automatically whenever new domains or addresses are blocked. Simply configure a refresh interval on your device, and the system will continuously sync the list, keeping your network always protected and up to date.

items.question: Does blocking affect network performance?
items.answer: No, blocking does not affect your network performance.

startGuide.title: Start Guide: Discovering Kratox.io
startGuide.subtitle: Discover how our platform protects your infrastructure and ensures legal compliance.

whatIs.title: What is Kratox.io?
whatIs.desc: Kratox.io is an advanced network protection and judicial compliance platform. Focused on ISPs and companies, it allows centralized management of DNS blocking and IP filtering with Artificial Intelligence, automating compliance with court orders and protecting against real-time threats.

dnsBlocking.title: DNS Blocking & AI
dnsBlocking.desc: DNS blocking is simplified by our AI. Instead of manual terminal commands, you can simply paste logs, emails, or upload PDF reports. Our AI automatically detects malicious domains and suggests immediate blocking, allowing you to approve suggestions with a single click.

ipBlocking.title: Simplified IP Filtering
ipBlocking.desc: IP detection works the same way: our intelligence identifies IP addresses and subnets (CIDR) in your documents and technical reports. Once identified, they can be blocked instantly at your network edge without any need for complex technical configuration by the user.

lists.title: Threat Feeds and Custom Lists
lists.desc: You can subscribe to global threat intelligence feeds or create your own manual lists, keeping your network one step ahead.

export.title: Export and Integration
export.desc: Kratox generates lists compatible with various equipment (MikroTik, Cisco, Juniper, Linux Bind/RPZ). You can download files manually or generate public links for automation.

links.title: Public Download Links
links.desc: Generate secure links with exclusive tokens so that your routers and DNS servers fetch the updated list automatically without the need for constant manual login.

cidr.title: CIDR Support
cidr.desc: Block entire subnets or specific IPs (/24, /32) with ease.

ai.title: AI Extraction
ai.desc: Identify IPs in PDF files or technical texts automatically.

sync.title: Direct Sync
sync.desc: Update blocklists at your network edge via API or secure link.

technicalDocs.title: Platform User Guide
technicalDocs.subtitle: Learn how to use analysis tools, feed management, and blocking automation.

aiAnalysis.title: AI Analysis & Extraction
aiAnalysis.desc: Use the 'Analyze Text' or 'Upload PDF' panels to process threat reports. The system will automatically extract domains and IPs (IOCs).
aiAnalysis.step1: Paste logs or upload PDF files.
aiAnalysis.step2: Review found items in the 'Suggested Domains' area.
aiAnalysis.step3: Click 'Block All' or add items individually.

feeds.title: Threat Feeds
feeds.desc: In the top panel, subscribe to managed lists. Updates from these lists are automatically applied to your account without manual intervention.

manual.title: Manual Management
manual.desc: Use the 'Add Manually' card to block specific domains, isolated IPs, or CIDR blocks (/24). Use the main table to search or remove items.

export.title: Data Export
export.desc: Click the 'Export List' button at the table. Choose formats like Hosts File, BIND9, Unbound, JSON, or CSV.

links.title: Automation Links
links.desc: Generate permanent public URLs for your equipment. Click 'Generate Link', choose the type (DNS or IP) and desired format (MikroTik, Cisco, etc). The link contains a secure token for automatic updates.

integrations.title: Integration Snippets
integrations.desc: Examples of how to configure your equipment to consume Kratox data.

codeComments.mikrotik: # Script to fetch list via Public Link

intro.title: Blocklist Automation
intro.subtitle: Complete guide to automating the consumption of the official blocked-domain list via Kratox download URL on your DNS server.
whyItems: The list is updated periodically, without manual work.
Your environment stays compliant with the lists provided by the platform.
The process is standardized and auditable, reducing operational errors.
intro.practice: In practice, the automation consists of: <bold>downloading the content from the URL</bold>, <bold>saving it into your DNS configuration file</bold> and <bold>safely validating/reloading the service</bold>.

prerequisites.title: Prerequisites
prerequisites.intro: Before you start, make sure you have:

items.title: Kratox Download URL
items.desc: Provided by the platform. The URL returns the list content (data), not a named file.

items.title: Administrative Access
items.desc: root/sudo access to the server where automation will be configured.

items.title: Linux Server with cron
items.desc: A Linux OS with a task scheduler (cron) enabled.

items.title: curl
items.desc: Command-line tool for downloading the content.

items.title: unbound
items.desc: Unbound DNS server (if applicable to your environment).

prerequisites.warning: This guide uses Unbound 1.19.2 on Ubuntu 24.04 as an example.

url.title: Step 1 — Understand what the URL delivers
url.desc: The Kratox URL works as a <bold>download endpoint</bold>. It returns the list content directly, which you will save locally as a file.
url.infoBox: Each customer has their own unique URL. The URL below is for illustration only:
url.testDesc: You can quickly check whether the URL responds with the commands below:
url.downloadComment: # Download and save manually (for inspection only)
url.checkComment: # Check if the URL responds (HTTP headers)

script.title: Step 2 — Create the update script
script.desc: The script below, developed for <bold>Unbound</bold>, automatically performs the following steps:
steps: Writes to the RPZ configuration file
script.permDesc: After creating the file, set the correct permissions:

scriptComments.url: # URL provided by Kratox (returns the list content)
scriptComments.dest: # Destination file on the server (example for Unbound)
scriptComments.download: # Download the content (the local output filename is defined by -o)
scriptComments.emptyCheck: # Fail if the downloaded content is empty
scriptComments.atomic: # Update the destination file safely (atomic) and with correct permissions

test.title: Step 3 — Test it manually
test.desc: Before scheduling it, run it manually to validate that everything works correctly:
test.infoBox: If there are no error messages in the terminal, the basic process is working correctly. You can confirm by checking whether the destination file was updated:

cron.title: Step 4 — Schedule the run (cron)
cron.desc: For production environments, a recommended approach is to create a dedicated file under /etc/cron.d/ — easier to maintain and audit than editing crontab directly.
cron.infoBox: The expression 0 * * * * means the script will run at minute 0 of every hour (i.e., every hour, such as 00:00, 01:00, 02:00, etc.).

answers: Ensure the destination directory and file are writable by root.
Check that the script is executable:
Confirm the path in cron and that the service exists:
Test the URL manually with curl -I and, if needed, with curl -v.
If the URL is expired or invalid, request a new URL/token in the platform.
This means some Unbound configuration is invalid. Run without redirecting output to see the full error message:
Also confirm that Unbound includes the generated RPZ file (according to your local configuration).

items.q: Unbound restart causes a brief impact
answers: This guide uses systemctl restart because it is simpler and more predictable. In sensitive environments, evaluate with your team whether to use systemctl reload unbound when applicable.

support.title: Support
support.desc: If you need additional help, check the <bold>Support</bold> section within the Kratox platform (customer panel), which typically includes:
readyItems: The URL you used (or token identification, when applicable)
The full error message (copied from the terminal)
Relevant DNS service logs (e.g., systemctl status output)
